notes: 1850. The Lex Loci Act of 1850 was an Indian law that allowed people who converted to a different faith to inherit their ancestral property. The Caste Disabilities Removal Act, 1850, was a law passed in British India under East India Company rule, that abolished all laws affecting the rights of people converting to another religion or caste. The new Act allowed hindus who converted from Hindu religion to another religion equal rights under new law, especially in the case of inheritance.

Three year. The elections to the two Houses of the Indian Legislature were held under the Government of India Act, 1919, and the new Legislative Assembly was inaugurated on 9 February 1921. The First and the Second Assemblies were dissolved within their life term of three years. The life of the Third, Fourth and the Fifth Assemblies were extended beyond their normal terms. The Fifth Assembly had the longest inning of ten years. The Sixth Assembly 1946-1947 was, in fact, not dissolved but automatically ceased to exist on 14 August 1947 under section 8 of the Indian Independence Act, 1947. Q.580. Which of the following Acts provided for a system of tribunals and a ban on strikes? SSC MTS 23/10/2024 (1st Shift) (a) The Trade Disputes Act, 1929 (b) Government of India Act, 1912 (c) Age of Consent Act, 1891 (d) Indian Partnership Act, 1932 Sol.580.(a) The Trade Disputes Act, 1929 was a law that aimed to resolve industrial disputes by establishing tribunals to investigate and settle them. Government of India Act, 1912 related to the appointment of a separate Governor of Bengal Province. The Age of Consent Act, raised the age for the consummation of marriage for women to 12 years. Indian Partnership Act, 1932 define and amend the Law Relating to Partnership.

Government of India Act 1858 had the following key provisions : The English East India Company was abolished, and India came directly under British Crown rule. A new position, the Secretary of State for India, was created in the British government to manage Indian affairs. A 15-member council was established to assist the Secretary of State.

a, b, c. The Indian Councils Act of 1909 was introduced by the British government in India as a step toward including Indians in governance. Named after Viceroy Lord Minto and Secretary of State John Morley, the Act considerably increased the size of the legislative councils, both at the Central and provincial levels. The number of members in the Central Legislative Council was raised from 16 to 60. The act declared Delhi as the capital of British India in place of Calcutta. Q.583. Wood’s Dispatch of primarily dealt with which of the following subject matters? SSC CGL 17/09/2024 (1st Shift) (a) Education (b) Railways (c) Finance (d) Health Sol.583.(a) Education. The Wood's Despatch of 1854 was a formal letter sent by Sir Charles Wood to Lord Dalhousie, the Governor-General of India, that outlined a plan for educational reform in India. It recommended that primary schools use vernacular languages, secondary schools use both English and vernacular languages, and colleges use English. It is often referred to as the “Magna Carta of English Education in India.”

Pitt’s India Act of 1784. It was introduced during the tenure of Lord Warren Hastings against the backdrop of growing concerns about the East India Company's mismanagement and corruption in India. Provisions: It gave the History British government control over the East India Company. The Act was also called ‘half-loaf system. Q.585. Which of the following Acts introduced ‘provincial autonomy’ by discontinuing the application of dyarchy? SSC CGL 18/09/2024 (3rd Shift) (a) Government of India Act, 1919 (b) Charter Act of 1813 (c) India Council Act, 1909 (d) Government of India Act, 1935 Sol.585.(d) Government of India Act, 1935. The Act introduced responsible government in provinces, meaning the governor was required to act on the advice of ministers responsible to the provincial legislature. Government of India Act, 1919: Introduced dyarchy. Charter Act of 1813: Ended the East India Company's trade monopoly and allowed Christian missionaries to promote moral and religious reforms in India.

Regulating Act. This Act was passed in June 1773. It was a response to the East India Company's semi-sovereign status in Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa, which was created after the battles of Plassey and Buxar. The act was intended to address the company's administration, but it wasn't a long-term solution. In 1784, the Pitt's India Act was passed as a more radical reform.

Rowlatt Act, 1919 : This Act passed on March 10, 1919, was introduced by British colonial authorities to suppress nationalist and revolutionary movements in India. The Act opposed by Mahatma Gandhi. India Contract Act, 1872: Regulates contracts and agreements in India. Criminal Tribes Act, 1871: Targeted "criminal tribes" and was repealed in 1952. Indian Slavery Act, 1843: Abolished slavery in British India.

Widow Remarriage. The Hindu Widow Remarriage Act of 1856 was enacted with the efforts of Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar. The draft of the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act, 1856 was prepared by Lord Dalhousie and passed by Lord Canning.

Indian Arms Act, 1878. It was enacted under the authority of Lord Lytton, the then Viceroy of India. It prohibited Indians from possessing, producing, or selling firearms without a government license. The Act was discriminatory as it did not apply to British, Anglo-Indians, Europeans, and certain government personnel. Q.590. Warren Hastings became the Governor-General of Bengal from Governor of Bengal after the passing of which of the following Acts ? SSC CHSL 09/07/2024 (4th shift) (a) Charter Act, 1813 (b) Pitt’s India Act, 1784 (c) Indian Council Act of 1861 (d) Regulating Act, 1773 Sol.590.(d) Regulating Act, 1773. The act was the first step taken by the British government to control and regulate the affairs of East India company in India.

1867. The Press and Registration of Books (PRB) Act of 1867 is a law that regulates the printing, publishing, and registration of books and newspapers in India. The Act was passed during the colonial era and was intended to limit free speech by imposing many procedural requirements for publishing.

China. Charter Act of 1833: This act marked the end of the company’s commercial operations, transitioning it into an administrative body overseeing British territories in India. This Act made the Governor-General of Bengal as the Governor-General of India. Lord William Bentinck - The first Governor - General of India.

Ilbert Bill. The Ilbert Bill, introduced by Sir Courtenay Ilbert in 1883, during the viceroyship of Lord Ripon. It sought to empower Indian judges to try British and European citizens in criminal cases. Q.594. By the Act of _________, the Governor General in Council was given the power to legislate for the whole of the British territories in India. Higher Secondary 20/06/2024 (Shift - 2) (a) Charter Act of 1833 (b) Charter Act of 1793 (c) Charter Act of 1853 (d) Charter Act of 1813 Sol.594.(a) Charter Act of 1833. It established the Governor General of Bengal as the Governor General of India, centralizing control over military and civil administration.

Tamil Nadu. The Ryotwari System (1820) was devised by Captain Alexander Read and Sir Thomas Munro. Under the Ryotwari system, the land revenue was paid by the farmers directly to the state. It was prevalent in most of southern India, first introduced in Tamil Nadu. It was later extended to Maharashtra, Berar, East Punjab, Coorg and Assam. Mahalwari System (1822) was devised by Holt Mackenzie.

1937, Indian provincial elections: Elections were held in eleven provinces - Madras, Central Provinces, Bihar, Orissa, the United Provinces, the Bombay Presidency, Assam, the North-West Frontier Province, Bengal, Punjab and Sind. The Indian National Congress (INC) won the majority of seats in the provincial elections, and formed governments in seven provinces.

Calcutta. Regulating Act 1773 - It was passed by the British Parliament to control the territories of the East India Company majorly in Bengal. The act provided for the appointment of a Governor-General along with four Councillors in the Presidency of Fort William (Calcutta), jointly called the Governor-General in Council. As per this, Warren Hastings was appointed as the Governor-General of the Presidency of Fort William (Bengal).

1915. The Defence of India Act (1915) - It was an emergency law that gave the British government in India wide powers to suppress dissent and maintain public order during World War I. It was passed by the then Governor General of India Lord Hardinge.

Act of 1947. The Indian Independence Act 1947 was an Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom that granted independence to British India. The Act was passed on July 18, 1947, and came into effect on August 15, 1947. The Act divided British India into two independent dominions, India and Pakistan.

1935. Government of India Act, 1935 : Contain three lists, Federal List (for Centre containing 59 items), Provincial List (for provinces containing 54 items) and the Concurrent List (for both containing 36 items).

1772. The system of Dual Government: Introduced in Bengal by Robert Clive of British East India Company. It lasted from 1765 to 1772. Under this system, the administration of Bengal was divided into Nizamat and Diwani. The Diwani was carried out by the company and the Nizamat by the Nizam. Q.603. Ryotwari system of revenue collection in India, introduced by the British, was based on the_______. SSC CGL 14/07/2023 (1st shift) (a) Smith's theory of rent (b) Ricardian theory of rent (c) Malthusian theory of rent (d) Marx's theory of rent Sol.603.(b) Ricardian theory of rent - The land of higher fertility gets higher rent than less fertile land. Ryotwari System - The peasants or cultivators were regarded as the owners of the land. The cultivators had to pay annual taxes directly to the government. Introduced by Thomas Munro and Alexander Read in in the Madras and Bombay presidency.

1858. Charter Act 1858 - The term Governor General changed to Viceroy for the British crown. The first viceroy was Lord Canning. The act ended ‘The Doctrine of Lapse’. It ended the Dual government scheme initiated due to Pitt’s India Act. Q.605. In 1942, the British government sent the___________mission to India. SSC CGL 26/07/2023 (4th shift) (a) Cripps (b) Linlithgow (c) Cabinet (d) Lansdowne Sol.605.(a) Cripps. It was sent to India to secure Indian cooperation for the British war efforts. It was headed by Sir Richard Stafford Cripps. Gandhi called it 'A post-dated cheque drawn on a failing bank'. Q.606. The result of the English Education Act of 1835 was to make ________ as a medium of instruction for ___________ education. SSC MTS 08/05/2023 (Morning) (a) vernacular, higher (b) English, primary (c) English, higher (d) vernacular, primary Sol.606.(c) English, Higher. English Education Act (1835) was proposed by William Bentinck on the Advice of Macaulay. Reforms by William Bentick : The abolition of Sati, the suppression of female infanticide and Thuggee, the abolition of lawlessness, and the abolition of human sacrifices.

20. The Charter Act of 1813 (The East Indian Company Act 1813) - Features: It ended the monopoly of the East India Company over commercial trade except trade with China and trade in tea with India. Q.608. The objective of Simon Commission was to________. SSC MTS 19/05/2023 (Morning) (a) decide India's Political future (b) decide India's Religious future (c) decide India's Educational future (d) decide India's economic future Sol.608.(a) Simon Commission - It was appointed by the British Government in 1927 under the chairmanship of John Simon (other members were Pathick Lawrence and Clement Atlee) to review the workings of Government of India (GOI) Act, 1919 and suggest measures. Q.609. Which act of the British Parliament was also known as East India Company Act 1793? SSC MTS 19/05/2023 (Morning) (a) The Government of India Act, 1793 (b) The Charter Act, 1793 (c) The Regulating Act, 1793 (d) The Indian Councils Act, 1793 Sol.609.(b) The Charter Act, 1793. The act was passed by the British Parliament to renew the charter of the East India Company to trade within India for next 20 years. It was passed during the tenure of Sir John Shore (1793 -1798). Q.610. In __________, the British government enacted a legislation banning the use of printed cotton textiles - chintz - in England. Interestingly, this Act was known as the Calico Act. SSC CHSL 14/03/2023 (3rd Shift) (a) 1720 (b) 1700 (c) 1740 (d) 1730 Sol.610.(a) 1720. The cotton textiles which the Portuguese took back to Europe, along with the spices, came to be called "calico". Calico Act , Reason - Textile makers in England were Unable to compete with Indian textiles. Highest cotton producing state (Gujarat).

1850. This law allowed Indians who had converted to Christianity to inherit the property of his ancestors. The Charter Act of 1813 allowed Chirstian missionaries to enter India and Preach Christianity. This Act asserted the Crown's sovereignty over British possessions in India.

Hyderabad. In this system, an Indian ruler had to maintain British troops in his state, either by giving some of his territories or by paying for the maintenance of the troops. It was first introduced by the French East India Company Governor Joseph Francois Dupleix, Later used by Lord Wellesley, Governor-General of India (1798 to 1805).

The Royal Titles Act 1876 was an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom which officially recognized Queen Victoria (and subsequent monarchs) as “Empress of India”. This act was introduced by Benjamin Disraeli. It was repealed on 22 June 1948.

Industrial disputes Act 1947. The Rowlatt Act 1919 authorized the arrest, for 2 years without trial, of any person suspected of terrorism living in British India. Indian Council acts (1861, 1892, and 1909). Charter Acts (1793, 1813, 1833, and 1853). Q.615. Which Act passed by British Government of India abolished the Council of India? SSC MTS 14/07/2022 (Afternoon) (a) Indian Independence Act of 1947 (b) India Council Act of 1892 (c) Government of India Act of 1935 (d) Charter Act of 1853 Sol.615.(c) Government of India Act were as follows: Abolition of provincial dyarchy and introduction of dyarchy at centre. Abolition of Indian Council and introduction of an advisory body in its place. Provision for an All India Federation with British India territories and princely states.
Sharda act. The act fixed the marriageable age for girls at 14 years and 18 years for boys. It was sponsor by Harbilas Sarda. Pitt’s India act (1784), Rowlatt Act (1919), Vernacular press act (1878).
1904. It was enacted during the time of Lord Curzon, Viceroy of India from 1899 to 1905 curbed the freedom of Press. Later this act was replaced by the Official Secrets act 1923.
Lord Lytton organized a durbar to acknowledge Queen Victoria as the Empress of India. This was the culmination of the transfer of control of British India from the East India Company to the Crown.

1911. The British government believed that ruling India from Delhi was easier and more convenient than from Calcutta. Therefore, Delhi was officially announced as the capital of British Raj by Emperor George V, on December 12, 1911.
